summary.text: Deterministic mock analysis of "Order service failing with database connection-pool exhaustion" based on 11 evidence item(s) affecting orders-service.
summary.impact: Severity was classified as high. Actual user/business impact should be confirmed against the evidence.

timeline.title: Marketing analytics dashboard
timeline.description: orders-service request volume up 35% starting 09:30 UTC, coinciding with a promotional email campaign sent to 400k subscribers at 09:28 UTC.

timeline.title: deploy-log-orders-service
timeline.description: orders-service deployed v5.2.0 at 09:35 UTC: refactored the repository layer to use request-scoped database sessions via the new ORM session manager. DB connection pool max size unchanged at 30 per pod. Also included minor logging improvements.

timeline.title: Datadog
timeline.description: Scheduled autovacuum began on the orders_history table at 09:41 UTC, elevating disk I/O wait on the orders-db replica (not the primary) for its usual ~40 minute duration.

timeline.title: orders-service.log
timeline.description: INFO: "session closed" log line frequency dropped from ~48/min (pre-deploy baseline) to ~11/min after 09:35 UTC, while "session opened" frequency stayed roughly constant.

timeline.title: orders-api-error.log
timeline.description: POST /api/orders -- 63 failures between 09:50 and 10:15 UTC. Error: 'connection pool exhausted, timed out waiting 5000ms for available connection'.

timeline.title: postgres-orders-primary.log
timeline.description: FATAL: remaining connection slots are reserved for non-replication superuser connections -- logged 19 times between 09:52 and 10:20 UTC.

timeline.title: Support ticket queue
timeline.description: 8 tickets between 09:55 and 10:20 UTC: most mention the order eventually going through on a second or third attempt, e.g. 'had to try 3 times but it worked eventually', 'checkout hung then failed, retried and it was fine'.

timeline.title: Datadog
timeline.description: orders-db connection-pool utilization climbed steadily from 42% at 09:40 UTC to 100% at 10:05 UTC, then stayed pinned near 100% -- a gradual rise over 25 minutes, not a sudden spike.

timeline.title: Incident report
timeline.description: orders-service has been intermittently returning 500 errors since roughly 09:40 UTC, with logs pointing to database connection-pool exhaustion. A deploy shipped shortly before reports started; a marketing campaign also drove a traffic increase around the same time.

facts.statement: orders-service.log reported: INFO: "session closed" log line frequency dropped from ~48/min (pre-deploy baseline) to ~11/min after 09:35 UTC, while "session opened" frequency stayed roughly constant.
facts.explanation: Directly observed in evidence item sample-db-connection-leak-ev-07 (application log).

facts.statement: Datadog reported: orders-db connection-pool utilization climbed steadily from 42% at 09:40 UTC to 100% at 10:05 UTC, then stayed pinned near 100% -- a gradual rise over 25 minutes, not a sudden spike.
facts.explanation: Directly observed in evidence item sample-db-connection-leak-ev-03 (monitoring alert).

facts.statement: Marketing analytics dashboard reported: orders-service request volume up 35% starting 09:30 UTC, coinciding with a promotional email campaign sent to 400k subscribers at 09:28 UTC.
facts.explanation: Directly observed in evidence item sample-db-connection-leak-ev-04 (monitoring alert).

facts.statement: Datadog reported: Scheduled autovacuum began on the orders_history table at 09:41 UTC, elevating disk I/O wait on the orders-db replica (not the primary) for its usual ~40 minute duration.
facts.explanation: Directly observed in evidence item sample-db-connection-leak-ev-08 (monitoring alert).

facts.statement: orders-api-error.log reported: POST /api/orders -- 63 failures between 09:50 and 10:15 UTC. Error: 'connection pool exhausted, timed out waiting 5000ms for available connection'.
facts.explanation: Directly observed in evidence item sample-db-connection-leak-ev-05 (api error).

facts.statement: postgres-orders-primary.log reported: FATAL: remaining connection slots are reserved for non-replication superuser connections -- logged 19 times between 09:52 and 10:20 UTC.
facts.explanation: Directly observed in evidence item sample-db-connection-leak-ev-06 (database error).

assumptions.statement: It is possible that this is related to the incident: orders-service deployed v5.2.0 at 09:35 UTC: refactored the repository layer to use request-scoped database sessions via the new ORM session manager. DB connection pool max size unchanged at 30 per pod. Also included minor logging improvements.
assumptions.explanation: Based on deployment note evidence item sample-db-connection-leak-ev-02; this has not been confirmed.

assumptions.statement: It is possible that this is related to the incident: 8 tickets between 09:55 and 10:20 UTC: most mention the order eventually going through on a second or third attempt, e.g. 'had to try 3 times but it worked eventually', 'checkout hung then failed, retried and it was fine'.
assumptions.explanation: Based on user report evidence item sample-db-connection-leak-ev-09; this has not been confirmed.

assumptions.statement: It is possible that this is related to the incident: This same connection-pool-utilization alert has fired sporadically 3-4 times over the past two months, always self-resolving within an hour with no deploy involved. Exact prior dates were not logged anywhere searchable.
assumptions.explanation: Based on support message evidence item sample-db-connection-leak-ev-10; this has not been confirmed.

assumptions.statement: It is possible that this is related to the incident: Per-request connection-hold-time metrics are not currently instrumented for orders-service, so it is not possible to directly confirm from existing dashboards whether individual requests are holding connections for longer than before the deploy.
assumptions.explanation: Based on other evidence item sample-db-connection-leak-ev-11; this has not been confirmed.

reasoningRisks.title: Deployment timing does not establish causation
reasoningRisks.description: A deployment or configuration change is present in the evidence for this incident. Timing alone does not confirm that it caused the incident.
reasoningRisks.mitigation: Look for a concrete causal mechanism (a specific config value, code change, or resource exhaustion), not just temporal proximity.

reasoningRisks.mitigation: Explicitly test whether this earlier evidence is causally connected to the incident, or whether it is a coincidental, unrelated signal.
